summary: The post examines Steven Ma, an ex-hedge fund manager who claims to have reverse-engineered Ivy League admissions algorithms and charges wealthy families up to $1.1 million for guaranteed college placement. Ma's company ThinkTank uses data-driven optimization to game both college admissions and the US News rankings system, treating education access as an arbitrage opportunity. O'Neil draws a parallel to financial regulation, arguing that complex systems inevitably advantage wealthy insiders who can afford to decode and exploit the rules. The piece questions whether the predictability of elite college admissions undermines meritocracy and suggests disclosure laws might help, while acknowledging they too could be gamed.
key_points: A former hedge fund manager has turned college admissions into a quantitative modeling problem, complete with probability-weighted contracts and money-back guarantees
The commodification of college coaching creates a two-tier system where wealthy families can essentially purchase algorithmic advantages
Complex admissions systems, like financial regulations, tend to benefit insiders who can afford to reverse-engineer them
Ma plans to scale his operation by purchasing completed applications and building an 'optimizer' for choosing activities and classes
The predictability of elite admissions suggests the meritocratic ideal may be illusory
money_quotes: It's a huge modeling war that you can pay to engage in.
This whole college coaching industry reminds me a lot of financial regulation.
We complicate the rules to the point where only very well-off insiders know exactly how to bypass the rules.
To the extent that getting into one of these 'top schools' actually does give young people access to power, influence, and success, it's alarming how predictable the whole process has become.
Here's a thought: maybe we should have disclosure laws about college coaching and prep? Or would those laws be gamed too?
key_insight: When systems designed to measure merit become complex enough to be reverse-engineered, they inevitably become tools for wealthy insiders to game, transforming meritocracy into plutocracy.
